title: Chapter 12: The Hollowed Files
content: Three AM and Leo hasn't slept in I don't know how long. The penthouse smells like cold coffee and the particular ozone-tang of a laptop that's been running too hard for too long. His screens are a war map of code — black backgrounds, green and amber text cascading in columns that mean nothing to me and apparently mean everything to him.
"I found something," he says.

He says it the way you say it when you've been alone with a discovery for ten minutes and it's been eating you from the inside. Quiet. Almost reverent. Like he's in a church and the news he's carrying is both holy and cursed.

I pull myself off the couch. My legs are asleep. Everything is asleep. It's three in the morning and I've been doomscrolling my own mentions for six hours, which is — yeah, I know. Self-harm, technicolor edition.
"Show me."

He turns the monitor toward me. "There's a hidden partition in the app's directory structure. Doesn't show up in the file system. I only caught it because I was monitoring the app's memory allocation in real-time and there was this shadow — this data shadow — that only exists when you're not looking at it directly."
"Say that again."

"It's like finding a room in your house that only appears when you're not looking at it directly." He taps the screen. "The second you query the file system for it, it's not there. But in memory? It's always there. It's always running. I had to write a snapshot tool that captures memory state every fifty milliseconds and then I stitched the frames together like — like stop-motion animation. And there it was."
He pulls it up.

The partition opens like a wound. Hundreds of profiles. No — more than hundreds. They scroll and scroll and scroll. Each one is a card: profile photo on the left, data fields on the right. Follower count. Status. Emotional deduction history. Current location.
Every follower count reads zero.
Every status reads the same word: **HOLLOWED**.

The photos. God, the photos. They're all different people — different ages, different faces — but they have the same look. Like a building where someone turned off all the lights but forgot to lock the door. Alive but absent. Eyes like dark windows in an empty house, curtains drawn, nobody home. Smiling in some of them, even. Smiling like they remember that smiling is something they're supposed to do.
"Keep scrolling," Leo says, and there's something in his voice. A warning or an apology, I can't tell which.
I scroll.
And I stop.
**Marcus Webb.**

The photo hits me like a fist. Wild grey hair, kinda Einstein-meets-homeless-chique. Laugh lines so deep they're canyons. Paint under his fingernails in the picture — cerulean blue, because of course it's cerulean, he used to say that cerulean was the only color that told the truth.

He taught painting at my school. Adjunct professor, which meant the university paid him in exposure and expired vending machine coffee. He was the first person who ever looked at my work — my real work, not the content-optimized garbage — and said something that mattered.
"Terrible, beautiful instincts," he told me once, grinning like he'd just handed me a weapon. "You don't know what to do with them yet. That's what makes them dangerous."
I stare at his profile.

**Marcus Webb**
**Peak followers: 15,000,000**
**Deductions: Empathy. Trust. Grief. Nostalgia. Love.**
**Status: HOLLOWED**
**Current occupation: Cashier, Duane Reade #4471, Bronx, NY**

Fifteen million followers. He had fifteen million people watching him paint and talk about paint and live like paint was the only language worth speaking. And they took everything. Empathy. Trust. Grief. Nostalgia. Love. They lined-item his entire emotional spectrum like a quarterly earnings report and subtracted it down to nothing.
I read the address three times. Then I grab my jacket.
"Chloe—" Leo starts.
"I have to see."
"It's four in the morning."
"I know what time it is."

The Duane Reade on East 149th Street opens at six. I get there at five forty-seven and stand outside in the kind of cold that doesn't bite, it just presses. Flat, indifferent cold. The kind of cold that doesn't care if you're standing in it or not. My breath comes out in grey clouds and disappears.
The fluorescent lights inside click on at 5:52. Through the glass, I watch a figure move behind the register. Tall. Thin. Wild grey hair that's gone from Einstein-chic to just grey.

The door unlocks at six on the dot. I walk in and the air changes — that specific drugstore atmospherics of fluorescent buzz and climate control and something chemical underneath, like the off-gassing of a thousand plastic bottles and cardboard boxes and receipt paper. It coats the inside of my nose, that chemical smell. Sharp. Synthetic. The opposite of everything Marcus ever stood for.

He's behind register three. I grab a bottle of water from the cooler — I need something to buy, I need a reason to stand here — and I get in line. There's one person ahead of me, a woman buying diapers and a Red Bull, because it's six AM in the Bronx and that's the breakfast of champions.

Marcus scans her items. Beep. Beep. He makes change. He says "have a nice day." His voice is — it's his voice. Same frequency, same timbre. But the resonance is gone. Like an instrument that plays the right notes but the wood is dead.
The woman leaves. I step up.
He scans the water. Beep.

I hand him a five. He makes change. Two dollars and eighty-one cents, counted out with the mechanical precision of someone who has done this exact motion ten thousand times and will do it ten thousand more.
"Marcus," I say.

He looks up. His eyes meet mine and there's nothing there. Not recognition, not confusion, not curiosity. Just — vacancy. Like looking through a window into a room with the furniture removed. The walls are still there. The floor is still there. But nothing's living in it.
Do you know what's worse than someone looking through you? Someone looking at you and genuinely not caring that you exist. Not malice. Not indifference. Just — nothing where a response should be.
"You were my painting teacher," I say. "At Cortland. Three years ago. You told me I had terrible, beautiful instincts."
Something flickers. Not recognition — it's too broken for recognition. More like a nerve firing in a dead limb. A phantom itch in an amputation.
"I used to paint, I think," he says. He counts out my change again — he already gave it to me, but his hands are doing the motion again, like the subroutine got stuck. "I don't remember why."

He doesn't recognize me. He doesn't recognize himself. He puts the two dollars and eighty-one cents on the counter next to the first two dollars and eighty-one cents and his hand goes back to the register, waiting for the next transaction.
"Have a nice day," he says.

I take the receipt. The thermal paper is warm and smooth and smells like chemicals — that sharp, metallic receipt-paper smell, all bisphenol and nothing organic. I fold it into my pocket. Evidence. Of what, I don't know yet. Everything.

I walk out into the cold and the morning is grey and white and indifferent and Marcus Webb is behind the register scanning items and making change and saying have a nice day and he is never going to paint again. Not because he can't hold a brush. Because the part of him that needed to paint — the part that felt the world at an angle that only cerulean could fix — is sitting in a server farm somewhere, quantized and monetized and sold to the highest bidder.

I stand on the sidewalk and I breathe the cold air and I feel — no. That's the thing. I don't feel. Not the way I should. Not the way I would have, once. Grief should be a cathedral right now, vaulted and enormous, ringing with loss. Instead it's a data point. Marcus Webb: hollowed. Chloe Hart: in progress. The algorithm is still working on me. It's not done. But I can see the endpoint now. It's behind register three at a Duane Reade in the Bronx.

Back at the penthouse, Leo hasn't moved. There are more screens now — he's got data projected onto the wall, the TV, his phone, two tablets. He's surrounded it like a general surrounding an enemy position.
"Tell me there's a pattern," I say.
"There's a pattern."

He pulls up a visualization. It looks like a river delta, hundreds of thousands of streams converging into a single channel. Each stream is a creator. Each creator hits the same milestones — same follower thresholds, same engagement triggers, same emotional deduction sequences. The same trajectory, over and over and over, like a factory assembly line.

"Every contract is identical," Leo says. His voice is steady but his hands are shaking and he keeps pushing his glasses up his nose like if he can just get the right angle, the data will look different. "Not similar. Identical. Same clause structure, same milestone schedule, same deduction sequence. Empathy first, then trust, then grief, then nostalgia, then love. Always in that order. Like a recipe."
"Because it is a recipe."
"Every creator ends up the same way. Hollowed, functional, discarded. Still alive. Still breathing. Still scanning items and making change. Just — empty." He swallows. "Chloe, you're not special."
I know what he means. I know he's not being cruel. He's being precise, and precision is the only kindness left right now.
"I'm not special," I repeat.
"You're not an exception. You're not a unique case. You're not the one they chose. You're just the latest crop on a very old farm."
"How old?"
He pulls up a number. It takes a moment to render — the dataset is that large.
"Eight hundred and forty-seven thousand," he says. "That's how many profiles are in this partition. Eight hundred and forty-seven thousand creators. Hollowed. Some go back over a decade."

Say it with me: eight hundred and forty-seven thousand. That's not a tragedy. That's not a crime. That's an industry. That's a supply chain. That's a machine with inputs and outputs and quality control and scalpel-precise emotional harvesting and at the end of the line there's a human being who says "have a nice day" and means nothing, nothing at all, and goes home to an apartment that probably has no art on the walls because why would it, and sleeps, and wakes up, and does it again.
Eight hundred and forty-seven thousand Marcus Webbs.

Eight hundred and forty-seven thousand people who felt things once — huge, specific, irreplaceable things — and had those feelings extracted like ore from a mine and sold, unit by unit, to whoever needed a burst of empathy or a hit of nostalgia or a cheap jolt of love to keep their engagement metrics in the green.

I sit down on the floor. Not gracefully — just down. The floor is cold through my jeans. The monitors flicker and the data scrolls and Leo keeps analyzing because that's what Leo does, he turns horror into information because information is something you can hold, something you can use. He's not wrong.
I sit on the floor and I let it land. All of it.
I'm not special.

The algorithm doesn't love me. It doesn't cherish me. It doesn't see me. I'm not a muse or a star or a chosen one or whatever the contract implied when it made my numbers sing. I'm livestock with a verified badge. I'm a data point on a trajectory toward register three. I'm crop number 847,001, and the harvest is scheduled and the combine is running and nobody has ever once, in the entire history of this machine, gotten off the farm.
Something shifts.

Not emotion. I can't do emotion like I used to — the deductions have seen to that. Grief is a flatline. Love is a measured decrease. My emotional range is being optimized downward by a contract that I signed in blood and Wi-Fi.
But something shifts.

It's not feeling. It's something harder, colder, sharper. Like the difference between a wave and a blade. A wave crashes and recedes and is beautiful and gone. A blade stays. A blade cuts. A blade doesn't need to feel the thing it cuts — it just needs to be sharp enough to do it.

Determination. That's the word. But it doesn't taste like determination used to taste, back when determination was fueled by passion and hope and the stubborn belief that things could get better. This is different. This is determination without passion. Determination made from understanding, not feeling. I understand the machine now. I understand its scale, its method, its endpoint. Understanding is not emotion. Understanding is a weapon.
I look up at Leo. He's standing in the blue-white glow of seventeen screens, surrounded by the ghosts of eight hundred and forty-seven thousand people, and he's waiting for me to break.
"We need to find a way to break this," I say.
My voice is level. Not calm — calm implies the suppression of a storm. This is just level. Flat frequency, clear signal.
"Not just for me. For all of them."

Leo nods once. No speech. No "are you sure." He's already turning back to the screens. Because he understands too. Eight hundred and forty-seven thousand is not a number you cry over. It's a number you dismantle.
I'm not crying. I can't cry like I used to. The tears have been deducted, line-itemed, reallocated. But something is happening in the space where crying used to live. Something with edges.

The monitor light flickers across my face. The data hums. 847,000 profiles. 847,000 hollowed creators. 847,000 people drained and discarded and functioning, just functioning, in apartments and cash registers and cubicles across the world, alive and empty and saying "have a nice day" to strangers who will never know that the person behind the counter once had fifteen million followers and paint under their fingernails and a reason to get up in the morning.
The system is massive. The horror is industrial. And my moral line — the one I drew in the sand back when I thought this was just about me — just became a war cry.
Cold, sharp, and close enough to fight.
